cards.definition: nutrients needed in large amounts — carbohydrates, proteins, and fats (lipids)
the main source of energy — simple (sugars) or complex (starch). Found in bread, rice, pasta, fruit, and sugar
needed for growth, repair, and maintenance of the body — made of amino acids. Found in meat, fish, eggs, and pulses
amino acids the body cannot make — must be obtained from food. Found in complete proteins like meat and eggs
protein containing all essential amino acids — found in animal sources: meat, fish, eggs, dairy
protein lacking one or more essential amino acids — found in plant sources: beans, lentils, cereals
combining LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids — e.g. beans on toast, rice and lentils
fat found mainly in animal products — associated with raised cholesterol and increased risk of heart disease
fat found mainly in plant oils and oily fish — associated with lower cholesterol. Includes monounsaturated and polyunsaturated
non-starch polysaccharide found in plant foods — aids digestion, prevents constipation and reduces risk of bowel cancer
nutrients needed in small amounts — vitamins and minerals
a water-soluble vitamin needed for healthy skin, immune function, and iron absorption — found in citrus fruit, peppers, and broccoli
a fat-soluble vitamin needed for calcium absorption and healthy bones — made by the skin in sunlight; found in oily fish and eggs
a mineral needed to make haemoglobin in red blood cells — deficiency causes anaemia. Found in red meat, spinach, and fortified cereals
a mineral needed for strong bones and teeth and muscle function — found in dairy products, leafy greens, and fortified plant milks
vitamins B and C — dissolve in water and cannot be stored in the body. Must be eaten regularly; easily lost in cooking
vitamins A, D, E, and K — stored in the liver. Can accumulate to toxic levels if consumed in excess
a UK government guide showing the proportions of different food groups needed for a healthy, balanced diet
UK government guidelines for the amounts of energy and nutrients needed by different groups of people
calculating the nutrient content of a dish or diet — used to evaluate whether it meets recommended intakes
the irreversible change to the structure of a protein when heated — e.g. egg white solidifying when cooked
the setting of protein when heated — e.g. eggs setting, meat firming up when cooked
starch grains absorb water and swell when heated, thickening the mixture — e.g. in sauces and custards
the combining of two liquids that normally separate — an emulsifier holds oil and water together (e.g. lecithin in egg yolk for mayonnaise)
the browning of sugar when heated to high temperatures — creates complex flavours
a browning reaction between amino acids and sugars when heated — creates flavour and colour in bread crusts, roast meat, and toast
illness caused by eating food contaminated with harmful bacteria — e.g. Salmonella, E. coli, Campylobacter
the temperature range 8°C–63°C in which bacteria multiply most rapidly — food should not be kept in this range
the transfer of bacteria from one food to another — e.g. using the same chopping board for raw meat and salad
a food safety management system identifying and controlling risks at critical points in food production
